item.title: I built a Plex server to escape streaming app fatigue — and it actually worked
item.itunes:title: I built a Plex server to escape streaming app fatigue — and it actually worked
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I'm done with cable, and now I'm through with streaming providers. Both were good while they lasted.

I recall the absolutely brilliant years of soaps in the evenings with family and earlier seasons of Big Brother UK to catch up with friends the next day in school. Like millions of other millennials, TV was a central part of my upbringing. But moving from TV to streaming landed me with the same problem: paying for multiple subscriptions. I wanted to save money, so I started a Plex server. I haven't turned back.

What went wrong with streaming?

The golden age is long gone

Television and cable channels have always involved switching between providers. Here in the UK, we'd purchase a TV magazine, detailing channel schedules for the week ahead, gather around the box set each evening, and enjoy some pre-recorded and live TV. That's all well and good until you bring in multiple broadcast providers and premium channels. Need access to a specific season of your favorite show? There's a good chance it's only available with select channels at set times.

We wanted better. We deserved better. This created the current streaming landscape, which started as a fresh new way to consume media. Instead of needing to tune in at specific times each day, you could enjoy your favorite movies and shows at your leisure. It was the dawn of a new age, but this would not last as more companies took notice of just how many people were willing to pay for monthly subscriptions to stream content just about everywhere. Enter the NAS and self-hosted streaming.

Cord-cutting no longer means cheaper TV. Streaming services require higher amounts regularly.

Network-attached storage (NAS) has become the answer. These powerful network storage boxes are more like compact servers and can run various apps and services, and store backups, media, documents, and more. One such service is Plex, a streaming service like Netflix, but there's a catch. Plex doesn't require you to pay a monthly fee, and you can use your own purchased media. Be it ripped Blu-ray discs or digital downloads, Plex can catalog and stream them all.

Cord-cutting no longer means cheaper TV. Streaming services regularly require higher amounts. They're also strategically providing specific content, making it more difficult to stick to just one and enjoy all the top shows. Instead of Disney launching all its content across the board for maximum exposure, the company opted to launch its own service. The same with Apple. If you want access to all available content and not miss a beat, be prepared to spend quite a small fortune.

How I replaced all these streaming services with one self-hosted app

Cutting the online streaming cord with this one free self-hosted app.

30
By  Rich Edmonds

Then there's bundling, which essentially made cable untenable for many. Streaming does the same with packages, sports add-ons, and more. That's what Plex (and other streaming packages) can help change, depending on what you like to enjoy. Cable bundled everything, Netflix launched to bring an end to this complexity, but studios decided to once again fragment everything, and bundles made a return with higher prices. Self-hosting your own streaming service is one solution.

Synology DiskStation DS925+
CPU
AMD Ryzen V1500B
Memory
4 GB DDR4 ECC SODIMM
Drive Bays
4 x HDD, 2 x NVMe
Expansion
1 x USB Type C
Ports
2 x 2.5GbE RJ-45; 2 x USB-A 3.2 Gen 1
OS
DSM 7.2

If you're serious about streaming content and have thousands of files to catalog and enjoy remotely, I highly recommend the Synology DiskStation DS925+. It's powerful, can take up to four 3.5-inch drives, and offers the incredibly user-friendly DSM OS.

Dimensions
166 mm x 199 mm x 223 mm
Weight
2.26 kg
See at Synology $640 at Amazon

Where Plex fits in with streaming

Making the most of your own content

The worst part about Amazon Prime Video, Netflix, and other options is the possibility that something you previously enjoyed viewing may no longer be available for streaming. Some listings might have a limit for how long they'll be available on a specific platform, depending on the deal between the service and the studio involved. This is fine if you're able to catch the media before it leaves the service, but what if you want to watch it again, and The Room is no longer available?

That's where Plex comes into play. Like other self-hosted streaming services, Plex can catalog all your stored media, be it shows, movies, or music. Everything is matched with online databases, pulling respective metadata and other accompanying content for a better viewing experience with trailers and integration with a wider media database. A NAS is a great way to start a media streaming platform such as Plex, and is precisely what I did in 2016, more effectively cutting the cord this time around.

​​​​​​​Making the move from streaming services to Plex is immediately liberating.

Making the move from streaming services to Plex is immediately liberating. Instead of wasting time scrolling through thousands of shows and movies, we can now purchase media we actually want to enjoy and add it to our Plex catalog. It doesn't take much to get Plex up and running, as the platform supports almost every modern CPU found inside prebuilt turnkey NAS solutions from Synology, TerraMaster, and other brands. All that's required are some drives large enough to contain the media collection (4K movies can be huge).

It's worth noting that what was available instantly reduced to a fraction of what a few streaming subscriptions would offer, but the media we purchased better match our tastes. I don't have much desire to sit through a few episodes of Love is Blind or Love Island, for instance. Streaming is handled locally, too. All that's required is to install the Plex app on a device that's on the same network as the NAS. I was able to get all our mobile hardware and TVs up and running with our media in minutes.

8 common Plex issues on NAS and how to fix them

Say goodbye to your Plex frustrations

3
By  Karandeep Singh

Performance is rock solid, especially within the LAN, but opening up external access is just as straightforward, thanks to Plex making use of its cloud platform. To sign in to Plex, you need to connect to its servers, which is a blessing and a curse. The former allows for seamless streaming wherever you are, but the latter keeps you tied to Plex's infrastructure. It's not truly local. That's when we eventually moved to Jellyfin to self-host all our media, but Plex is definitely an option worth considering to leave streaming services.

Plex
OS
Windows, macOS, Linux
Individual pricing
Free, $6.99/month, $250/lifetime

Plex is the premier home media server software for replacing your streaming subscriptions.

See at Plex

It's taught me to love media again

Owning something feels empowering

Credit: Source: Wikimedia Commons

Like replacing what seemed like a lifetime of rolling vehicle PCPs with a single purchase, owning media once again felt unexpectedly pleasant. I streamed through Spotify, having used the music service when it was first introduced to the UK as a beta in early 2009. It was brilliant to have unlimited access to a wide collection of tracks spanning multiple genres and periods. What was lost was that feeling of owning a particular album or track. I recall purchasing vinyl, cassettes, and CDs from local shops and knowing that I physically owned that copy. Much like gaming, this feeling is lost with streaming.

Deals

Big savings on NAS and drives — deals for media servers

Explore deals and discounts on network-attached storage, hard drives, switches, and routers to build a home media server. Find savings on NAS enclosures, backup drives, and networking gear plus accessories to cut streaming costs and keep media accessible.
Deals Explore Storage & Networking Deals

One could argue streaming media has flattened it into an endless feed, like scrolling through Reddit or Instagram for a good 30 minutes while in the loo. There's always some algorithm in the background, attempting to feed you with the right content at the right time to keep you engaged and increase the monthly active session count for shareholders. Your time is the most precious and valuable commodity to trade, and I often felt like I wasted it, getting nothing in return, by scrolling for something to watch on Netflix or Amazon rather than knowing precisely what we wanted to watch.

We're increasingly paying regularly for access to various things we used to own. Whether it's a vehicle, music album, or video game. There's something chilling to consider that there could be a point where we don't own anything. Everything is a subscription, even heated seats in some cars, and no, that's not a joke. Ownership is inconvenient until certainty is desired. Plex works because it restores a balance between convenience and ownership by bringing together the best features of streaming services and matching them to your own purchased content. So, I didn't really leave modern media; I just molded it to make it feel less exhausting.

Plex, Jellyfin, Emby, and other similar platforms are all great for replacing streaming services, truly cutting the cord, and saving a considerable sum of money each month. Instead of sending more than $20 per month to all these services, we use that to buy albums, movies, and show sets to catalog and enjoy whenever we want. They'll always be available, and classified sites and yard sales can be a goldmine for grabbing physical copies for the home streaming server.

3 key features where Plex still has the edge over Jellyfin — for now

Jellyfin is starting to catch up with the self-hosting giant, though.

12
By  Patrick O'Rourke

I recall the absolutely brilliant years of soaps in the evenings with family and earlier seasons of Big Brother UK to catch up with friends the next day in school. Like millions of other millennials, TV was a central part of my upbringing. But moving from TV to streaming landed me with the same problem: paying for multiple subscriptions. I wanted to save money, so I started a Plex server. I haven't turned back.

What went wrong with streaming?

The golden age is long gone

Television and cable channels have always involved switching between providers. Here in the UK, we'd purchase a TV magazine, detailing channel schedules for the week ahead, gather around the box set each evening, and enjoy some pre-recorded and live TV. That's all well and good until you bring in multiple broadcast providers and premium channels. Need access to a specific season of your favorite show? There's a good chance it's only available with select channels at set times.

We wanted better. We deserved better. This created the current streaming landscape, which started as a fresh new way to consume media. Instead of needing to tune in at specific times each day, you could enjoy your favorite movies and shows at your leisure. It was the dawn of a new age, but this would not last as more companies took notice of just how many people were willing to pay for monthly subscriptions to stream content just about everywhere. Enter the NAS and self-hosted streaming.

Cord-cutting no longer means cheaper TV. Streaming services require higher amounts regularly.

Network-attached storage (NAS) has become the answer. These powerful network storage boxes are more like compact servers and can run various apps and services, and store backups, media, documents, and more. One such service is Plex, a streaming service like Netflix, but there's a catch. Plex doesn't require you to pay a monthly fee, and you can use your own purchased media. Be it ripped Blu-ray discs or digital downloads, Plex can catalog and stream them all.

Cord-cutting no longer means cheaper TV. Streaming services regularly require higher amounts. They're also strategically providing specific content, making it more difficult to stick to just one and enjoy all the top shows. Instead of Disney launching all its content across the board for maximum exposure, the company opted to launch its own service. The same with Apple. If you want access to all available content and not miss a beat, be prepared to spend quite a small fortune.

How I replaced all these streaming services with one self-hosted app

Cutting the online streaming cord with this one free self-hosted app.

30
By  Rich Edmonds

Then there's bundling, which essentially made cable untenable for many. Streaming does the same with packages, sports add-ons, and more. That's what Plex (and other streaming packages) can help change, depending on what you like to enjoy. Cable bundled everything, Netflix launched to bring an end to this complexity, but studios decided to once again fragment everything, and bundles made a return with higher prices. Self-hosting your own streaming service is one solution.

Synology DiskStation DS925+
CPU
AMD Ryzen V1500B
Memory
4 GB DDR4 ECC SODIMM
Drive Bays
4 x HDD, 2 x NVMe
Expansion
1 x USB Type C
Ports
2 x 2.5GbE RJ-45; 2 x USB-A 3.2 Gen 1
OS
DSM 7.2

If you're serious about streaming content and have thousands of files to catalog and enjoy remotely, I highly recommend the Synology DiskStation DS925+. It's powerful, can take up to four 3.5-inch drives, and offers the incredibly user-friendly DSM OS.

Dimensions
166 mm x 199 mm x 223 mm
Weight
2.26 kg
See at Synology $640 at Amazon

Where Plex fits in with streaming

Making the most of your own content

The worst part about Amazon Prime Video, Netflix, and other options is the possibility that something you previously enjoyed viewing may no longer be available for streaming. Some listings might have a limit for how long they'll be available on a specific platform, depending on the deal between the service and the studio involved. This is fine if you're able to catch the media before it leaves the service, but what if you want to watch it again, and The Room is no longer available?

That's where Plex comes into play. Like other self-hosted streaming services, Plex can catalog all your stored media, be it shows, movies, or music. Everything is matched with online databases, pulling respective metadata and other accompanying content for a better viewing experience with trailers and integration with a wider media database. A NAS is a great way to start a media streaming platform such as Plex, and is precisely what I did in 2016, more effectively cutting the cord this time around.

​​​​​​​Making the move from streaming services to Plex is immediately liberating.

Making the move from streaming services to Plex is immediately liberating. Instead of wasting time scrolling through thousands of shows and movies, we can now purchase media we actually want to enjoy and add it to our Plex catalog. It doesn't take much to get Plex up and running, as the platform supports almost every modern CPU found inside prebuilt turnkey NAS solutions from Synology, TerraMaster, and other brands. All that's required are some drives large enough to contain the media collection (4K movies can be huge).

It's worth noting that what was available instantly reduced to a fraction of what a few streaming subscriptions would offer, but the media we purchased better match our tastes. I don't have much desire to sit through a few episodes of Love is Blind or Love Island, for instance. Streaming is handled locally, too. All that's required is to install the Plex app on a device that's on the same network as the NAS. I was able to get all our mobile hardware and TVs up and running with our media in minutes.

8 common Plex issues on NAS and how to fix them

Say goodbye to your Plex frustrations

3
By  Karandeep Singh

Performance is rock solid, especially within the LAN, but opening up external access is just as straightforward, thanks to Plex making use of its cloud platform. To sign in to Plex, you need to connect to its servers, which is a blessing and a curse. The former allows for seamless streaming wherever you are, but the latter keeps you tied to Plex's infrastructure. It's not truly local. That's when we eventually moved to Jellyfin to self-host all our media, but Plex is definitely an option worth considering to leave streaming services.

Plex
OS
Windows, macOS, Linux
Individual pricing
Free, $6.99/month, $250/lifetime

Plex is the premier home media server software for replacing your streaming subscriptions.

See at Plex

It's taught me to love media again

Owning something feels empowering

Credit: Source: Wikimedia Commons

Like replacing what seemed like a lifetime of rolling vehicle PCPs with a single purchase, owning media once again felt unexpectedly pleasant. I streamed through Spotify, having used the music service when it was first introduced to the UK as a beta in early 2009. It was brilliant to have unlimited access to a wide collection of tracks spanning multiple genres and periods. What was lost was that feeling of owning a particular album or track. I recall purchasing vinyl, cassettes, and CDs from local shops and knowing that I physically owned that copy. Much like gaming, this feeling is lost with streaming.

Deals

Big savings on NAS and drives — deals for media servers

Explore deals and discounts on network-attached storage, hard drives, switches, and routers to build a home media server. Find savings on NAS enclosures, backup drives, and networking gear plus accessories to cut streaming costs and keep media accessible.
Deals Explore Storage & Networking Deals

One could argue streaming media has flattened it into an endless feed, like scrolling through Reddit or Instagram for a good 30 minutes while in the loo. There's always some algorithm in the background, attempting to feed you with the right content at the right time to keep you engaged and increase the monthly active session count for shareholders. Your time is the most precious and valuable commodity to trade, and I often felt like I wasted it, getting nothing in return, by scrolling for something to watch on Netflix or Amazon rather than knowing precisely what we wanted to watch.

We're increasingly paying regularly for access to various things we used to own. Whether it's a vehicle, music album, or video game. There's something chilling to consider that there could be a point where we don't own anything. Everything is a subscription, even heated seats in some cars, and no, that's not a joke. Ownership is inconvenient until certainty is desired. Plex works because it restores a balance between convenience and ownership by bringing together the best features of streaming services and matching them to your own purchased content. So, I didn't really leave modern media; I just molded it to make it feel less exhausting.

Plex, Jellyfin, Emby, and other similar platforms are all great for replacing streaming services, truly cutting the cord, and saving a considerable sum of money each month. Instead of sending more than $20 per month to all these services, we use that to buy albums, movies, and show sets to catalog and enjoy whenever we want. They'll always be available, and classified sites and yard sales can be a goldmine for grabbing physical copies for the home streaming server.

3 key features where Plex still has the edge over Jellyfin — for now

Jellyfin is starting to catch up with the self-hosting giant, though.

12
By  Patrick O'Rourke

item.title: I built my own Synology alternative, and the hardest part was accepting how good DSM still is
item.itunes:title: I built my own Synology alternative, and the hardest part was accepting how good DSM still is
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Synology network-attached storage (NAS) enclosures are among the best in the business, thanks in part to the operating system running on the turnkey devices. Synology DiskStation Manager (DSM) is one of my favorite prebuilt NAS systems, as well as a shortlisted pick for best overall NAS OS. It's absolutely brilliant for most people using NAS. While not perfect, often limiting enthusiasts and those seeking more advanced functionality, it's ideal for beginners and those who simply wish to use their NAS to store some files and run a package or two. I have since moved my primary NAS to a custom DIY system, but I miss how amazing DSM still is.

Synology's DSM OS is something special

It's the best for a very good reason

Starting out with your first NAS can prove challenging, if not daunting. Many buying a prebuilt enclosure likely haven't used one before, aren't too tech-savvy, or were recommended to do so by someone with NAS experience. Synology makes some of the best enclosures for beginners, spanning from the super-affordable to the prosumer, providing the means to pick an enclosure for a wide range of applications and price points, as well as offering a clear upgrade route. Some competitors try to offer similar experiences, but it's often the hardware, software, or both that lack compared to Synology.

After years of invested R&D and an Apple-like approach, Synology has created the ultimate NAS ecosystem with hardware, software, and cloud-backed services to create a well-rounded storage and self-hosting platform. Buying even one of the most affordable DiskStation NAS will bag you access to the excellent DSM OS, the same software running more capable and expensive storage solutions. The DS223j may be a little old now, with three years under its belt, but the two-bay NAS has everything you need to stash some files and run some self-hosted apps.

I've tried many NAS brands, but I keep coming back to Synology — here's why

There's no place like a Synology home...

12
By  Rich Edmonds

It's painlessly easy to use, regardless of which DiskStation is purchased. That DS223j can be configured for external access with reverse proxies, self-hosted services, and more within an hour. Everything is well laid out, and optionally utilizing Synology's cloud backend makes it possible to open up your NAS for remote access without spending time working out how to do it manually. This can prove invaluable for things like Plex or Jellyfin, where remote access can allow you to save a lot on streaming services by using purchased content and running your own streaming platform.

Synology DiskStation DS223j
Brand
Synology
CPU
Realtek RTD1619B
Memory
1GB DDR4 (max. 1GB)
Drive Bays
2
Expansion
No
Ports
1x 1GbE, 1x USB-A 3.2 Gen 1

The Synology DiskStation DS223j is an affordable entry point for those looking to buy their very first NAS enclosure. It has an ARM processor, two drive bays and some ports to hook it up to your network.

Caching
No
OS
DSM
Price
200
Dimensions
165 x 100 x 225.5 mm
Weight
0.88 kg
$195 at Amazon $220 at Newegg

DSM is easier than other NAS OS

Do you even RAID, bro?

Setting up your first NAS can be daunting. It was for me back in 2009 with FreeNAS. Not only was it notoriously difficult and messy to use, but actual end users found it convoluted to get anything done. Sure, this was largely using the server as a file depot with SMB access, but it still wasn't great to use, and there wasn't a sleek web UI for everyone to interact with. Then there's reliability, which was satisfactory at best and downright infuriating at worst. I had to configure the server to automatically reboot every 12 hours to avoid connectivity issues on the FreeNAS side. It just wasn't great.

Then there's DSM, which allows you to go from zero to hero in minutes. With a few clicks after setting up the NAS and logging into DSM, you'll have a storage pool configured, shared folders up and running, users added, and file services enabled. There's a helpful on-board wizard that runs through everything DSM has to offer at the top level, allowing for some exploration to get accustomed to how everything is laid out and works. For those who want a NAS to be working and relatively boring to manage, a Synology DiskStation NAS with DSM running is about as good as you can get.

Compare it to a custom NAS running TrueNAS SCALE, and there's a world of difference. Sure, I can do much more with my own hardware and the OS of my choosing, but DSM takes much of the maintenance away. With DSM and a Synology NAS, you're effectively in control but with the backing of the brand. It's almost impossible to mess the NAS up badly enough that it stops working, and there are official support channels available for reaching out for assistance. With DIY NAS, you're on your own. Remote access simply works, and Package Center is a little underrated, especially by yours truly.

Proxmox running Xpenology gave me the best of both worlds for my home NAS

Synology > TrueNAS > Synology.

By  Rich Edmonds

The Apple approach works

Especially if you have a Mac and iPhone

Synology's approach to NAS and other endeavors may feel Apple-like, but I mean that as praise and criticism. Synology has been in the news recently regarding restrictions on storage drives that would be supported by new models. The company has since laxed those restrictions, but it left a sour taste, forcing customers to use Synology-branded drives for maximum margin. To die-hard fans, this isn't an issue, but to the average person, this could cause issues that those with Apple products know so well. Once you're inside the walled garden, you're in for the long run.

It's why many enthusiasts outgrow Synology, but there are positives to this business model. Firstly, hardware, software, cloud services, and everything else are tightly controlled and work together flawlessly. Though Synology's hardware can usually lag behind the competition and may not be worth the MSRP, it's heavily optimized through DSM to work better than it should. It's also the reason why DSM is so good. Synology has the available cash to re-invest in its software development arm. But it's the confidence that DSM strives to provide, especially to newcomers.

Knowing it'll work flawlessly

Confidence must be present when moving from cloud storage to local NAS. People have to believe their data will remain safe, both from outside malicious parties, accidental damage, and mismanagement. Synology's DSM Excels at all three (and then some). It really is one part of the Synology ecosystem I miss the most, which led me down trying out Xpenology on custom hardware to see if I can work in Synology's excellent software developments with my own parts. The result? I'd love for Synology to charge me for its software with official support.

Subscribe to the newsletter for DSM and NAS insights

Join the newsletter for deep, practical coverage of Synology DSM, NAS setup and troubleshooting, package recommendations, and broader storage/self-hosting analysis — expert-led context to help you choose and run the right setup.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

It'll never happen, but DSM truly is one of the best software packages out there for storing data.

item.content:encoded:
Synology network-attached storage (NAS) enclosures are among the best in the business, thanks in part to the operating system running on the turnkey devices. Synology DiskStation Manager (DSM) is one of my favorite prebuilt NAS systems, as well as a shortlisted pick for best overall NAS OS. It's absolutely brilliant for most people using NAS. While not perfect, often limiting enthusiasts and those seeking more advanced functionality, it's ideal for beginners and those who simply wish to use their NAS to store some files and run a package or two. I have since moved my primary NAS to a custom DIY system, but I miss how amazing DSM still is.

Synology's DSM OS is something special

It's the best for a very good reason

Starting out with your first NAS can prove challenging, if not daunting. Many buying a prebuilt enclosure likely haven't used one before, aren't too tech-savvy, or were recommended to do so by someone with NAS experience. Synology makes some of the best enclosures for beginners, spanning from the super-affordable to the prosumer, providing the means to pick an enclosure for a wide range of applications and price points, as well as offering a clear upgrade route. Some competitors try to offer similar experiences, but it's often the hardware, software, or both that lack compared to Synology.

After years of invested R&D and an Apple-like approach, Synology has created the ultimate NAS ecosystem with hardware, software, and cloud-backed services to create a well-rounded storage and self-hosting platform. Buying even one of the most affordable DiskStation NAS will bag you access to the excellent DSM OS, the same software running more capable and expensive storage solutions. The DS223j may be a little old now, with three years under its belt, but the two-bay NAS has everything you need to stash some files and run some self-hosted apps.

I've tried many NAS brands, but I keep coming back to Synology — here's why

There's no place like a Synology home...

12
By  Rich Edmonds

It's painlessly easy to use, regardless of which DiskStation is purchased. That DS223j can be configured for external access with reverse proxies, self-hosted services, and more within an hour. Everything is well laid out, and optionally utilizing Synology's cloud backend makes it possible to open up your NAS for remote access without spending time working out how to do it manually. This can prove invaluable for things like Plex or Jellyfin, where remote access can allow you to save a lot on streaming services by using purchased content and running your own streaming platform.

Synology DiskStation DS223j
Brand
Synology
CPU
Realtek RTD1619B
Memory
1GB DDR4 (max. 1GB)
Drive Bays
2
Expansion
No
Ports
1x 1GbE, 1x USB-A 3.2 Gen 1

The Synology DiskStation DS223j is an affordable entry point for those looking to buy their very first NAS enclosure. It has an ARM processor, two drive bays and some ports to hook it up to your network.

Caching
No
OS
DSM
Price
200
Dimensions
165 x 100 x 225.5 mm
Weight
0.88 kg
$195 at Amazon $220 at Newegg

DSM is easier than other NAS OS

Do you even RAID, bro?

Setting up your first NAS can be daunting. It was for me back in 2009 with FreeNAS. Not only was it notoriously difficult and messy to use, but actual end users found it convoluted to get anything done. Sure, this was largely using the server as a file depot with SMB access, but it still wasn't great to use, and there wasn't a sleek web UI for everyone to interact with. Then there's reliability, which was satisfactory at best and downright infuriating at worst. I had to configure the server to automatically reboot every 12 hours to avoid connectivity issues on the FreeNAS side. It just wasn't great.

Then there's DSM, which allows you to go from zero to hero in minutes. With a few clicks after setting up the NAS and logging into DSM, you'll have a storage pool configured, shared folders up and running, users added, and file services enabled. There's a helpful on-board wizard that runs through everything DSM has to offer at the top level, allowing for some exploration to get accustomed to how everything is laid out and works. For those who want a NAS to be working and relatively boring to manage, a Synology DiskStation NAS with DSM running is about as good as you can get.

Compare it to a custom NAS running TrueNAS SCALE, and there's a world of difference. Sure, I can do much more with my own hardware and the OS of my choosing, but DSM takes much of the maintenance away. With DSM and a Synology NAS, you're effectively in control but with the backing of the brand. It's almost impossible to mess the NAS up badly enough that it stops working, and there are official support channels available for reaching out for assistance. With DIY NAS, you're on your own. Remote access simply works, and Package Center is a little underrated, especially by yours truly.

Proxmox running Xpenology gave me the best of both worlds for my home NAS

Synology > TrueNAS > Synology.

By  Rich Edmonds

The Apple approach works

Especially if you have a Mac and iPhone

Synology's approach to NAS and other endeavors may feel Apple-like, but I mean that as praise and criticism. Synology has been in the news recently regarding restrictions on storage drives that would be supported by new models. The company has since laxed those restrictions, but it left a sour taste, forcing customers to use Synology-branded drives for maximum margin. To die-hard fans, this isn't an issue, but to the average person, this could cause issues that those with Apple products know so well. Once you're inside the walled garden, you're in for the long run.

It's why many enthusiasts outgrow Synology, but there are positives to this business model. Firstly, hardware, software, cloud services, and everything else are tightly controlled and work together flawlessly. Though Synology's hardware can usually lag behind the competition and may not be worth the MSRP, it's heavily optimized through DSM to work better than it should. It's also the reason why DSM is so good. Synology has the available cash to re-invest in its software development arm. But it's the confidence that DSM strives to provide, especially to newcomers.

Knowing it'll work flawlessly

Confidence must be present when moving from cloud storage to local NAS. People have to believe their data will remain safe, both from outside malicious parties, accidental damage, and mismanagement. Synology's DSM Excels at all three (and then some). It really is one part of the Synology ecosystem I miss the most, which led me down trying out Xpenology on custom hardware to see if I can work in Synology's excellent software developments with my own parts. The result? I'd love for Synology to charge me for its software with official support.

Subscribe to the newsletter for DSM and NAS insights

Join the newsletter for deep, practical coverage of Synology DSM, NAS setup and troubleshooting, package recommendations, and broader storage/self-hosting analysis — expert-led context to help you choose and run the right setup.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

It'll never happen, but DSM truly is one of the best software packages out there for storing data.

item.title: Claude Code is the best AI coding tool I've used, and I’m not entirely sure if I can recommend it
item.itunes:title: Claude Code is the best AI coding tool I've used, and I’m not entirely sure if I can recommend it
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
There was once a time when you'd only hear "technical people" in the traditional sense talk about coding or building stuff. No-code building tools always lingered around, but compared to what we have now, the tools that existed back then were simply toys. Now, even the most non-technical person you might have around is constantly talking about the app they're "vibe coding" on the weekend, the SaaS they're spinning up with Cursor, or the side project they finally shipped because an LLM wrote 90% of it for them.

AI-assisted coding tools have effectively removed the barrier that once existed when it came to coding, and Claude Code is the tool that, for me (like many), sits at the top of the pile. I've cycled through all the alternatives, and I always find myself coming back to Claude Code. The way it reasons through a codebase, plans multi-step changes, and actually ships working code with minimal hand-holding is something else. Anthropic has the most impressive LLMs when it comes to coding, and Claude Code is the harness that squeezes the most out of them. And yet, despite all of that, I'm not entirely sure I can recommend it.

Want to stay in the loop with the latest in AI? The XDA AI Insider newsletter drops weekly with deep dives, tool recommendations, and hands-on coverage you won't find anywhere else on the site. Subscribe by modifying your newsletter preferences!

The limits are brutal, and they keep getting worse

And I'm on the $100 plan

I wrote an entire article about this just a few days ago, but the biggest reason why I'm struggling to recommend Claude Code to anyone nowadays is the brutal limits it comes with. Plus, Anthropic seems adamant on making the paid subscriptions worse, and while I can't blame them entirely for it because demand has clearly outpaced what their infrastructure can comfortably handle, that doesn't make the experience any less frustrating for the people actually paying for it.

I'm subscribed to Claude's $100 Max tier, and I've tried using the $20 Pro tier. I hit limits on the former, and it's the plan I've been daily-driving for months, so I can't imagine how frustrating it must be for someone on the Pro tier trying to get any real work done. If I'm running out of usage on a plan that costs five times more, the people paying $20 a month are basically getting a glorified demo (well, people say that a $20 plan is now the new "free" tier). Unfortunately, it's not like the Pro tier was always this restrictive. The limits have tightened over time, and I feel like they keep getting worse. This is despite the fact that Anthropic keeps doubling rate limits and also recently announced a partnership with SpaceX to secure more compute.

I switched from Claude Code to Codex for a week, and the trade-offs surprised me

One week, two tools, a lot of opinions.

28
By  Mahnoor Faisal

Just yesterday, I was working on improving a project for a course I'm taking this semester, and I ended up hitting my hourly limit just an hour and a half into my session. I wasn't really doing anything particularly intensive — just some back-and-forth refactoring. I was then locked out for the next five hours, with no way to continue unless I shelled out for extra usage on top of what I'm already paying. I ended up jumping to Codex, and despite being on the $20 plan over there, I managed to get a lot more done!

This has been happening more and more frequently over the past few months, to the point where I've started planning my coding sessions around when my limits reset. That's a ridiculous way to use a tool that's supposed to make you faster, not force you to schedule your workflow around an arbitrary timer.

Claude Code alternatives keep getting better

You have options now

Given that Claude's limits have been frustrating me a fair bit lately, I've been exploring alternatives more and more. Now, for a very long time, Claude Code was truly the best terminal-based AI coding tool out there, with nothing else even coming close. We had a lot of coding agents with IDEs, and a few full-fledged agentic IDEs showing up too, but nothing that quite matched the experience of a proper terminal-based agent. However, that's no longer the case, and the space has gotten significantly more crowded over the past few months.

OpenAI's Codex is an excellent example. When I first tried it out when it was fairly new, I was disappointed and switched back to Claude Code immediately. However, I've been leaning toward it more and more lately, and I keep walking away wondering why I haven't been using it more often. OpenAI has been launching banger after banger feature, and Codex has quietly become a really compelling alternative. The recent additions, the model improvements, and the fact that it's actually included on the free plan too all add up to something that's genuinely hard to ignore. Codex's limits are also nowhere near as bad as Claude Code's are right now.

Then there's OpenCode, which is the alternative I'd point most people toward right now. It's completely free, completely open-source, and the biggest selling point is that it lets you bring your own model. You can hook it up to most popular LLMs out there or even a local LLM running on your own machine. OpenCode has almost every feature that makes Claude Code well, Claude Code, including a Plan mode, Skills, agents, MCP servers, and so on.

The harness might not be as polished as Claude Code's, but you lose some to gain some. What you get in return is a level of flexibility that Claude Code simply can't offer at the moment. You're not tied to a single provider, you're not at the mercy of whatever pricing changes Anthropic decides to roll out next, and you're not going to find yourself locked out for five hours in the middle of an important task. If a new model drops next week that's better at coding than Claude, you just point OpenCode at it and keep going!

There are other alternatives worth mentioning too, and my colleagues here at XDA have constantly talked about them. That said, Codex and OpenCode are the two I keep coming back to when I think about what I'd genuinely recommend over Claude Code today.

There's also the closed-source problem

Open-source isn't just a buzzword

Now, this isn't an issue that really affects me specifically, since I don't work as a software developer professionally. However, with how great open-source Claude Code alternatives and local LLMs have gotten, it's become harder to ignore that Claude Code is, at the end of the day, a closed-source tool tied to a single company's cloud. If you're working on anything sensitive like proprietary code, client work under NDA, security research, or just anything you'd rather not send off to someone else's servers, Claude Code isn't really the best option.

Subscribe to the AI Insider newsletter for hands-on tool analysis

Dig deeper by subscribing to the AI Insider newsletter for rigorous deep dives, practical tool recommendations, and hands-on coverage of AI coding tools, their limits, and open-source alternatives so you can evaluate and pick the right tooling.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Even if Anthropic's data handling is great (and I have no reason to believe it isn't), it's closed-source at the end of the day. This sort of brings me back to OpenCode, which is the best open-source Claude Code alternative I can recommend. It's fully open-source, which means anyone can read the code, audit it, and see exactly what it's doing. And because it lets you bring your own model, you can pair it with a local LLM running on your own machine and have a setup where your code literally never leaves your network.

Might be my favorite, but favorites can change

I'm a Claude fan through and through, but I've found it increasingly difficult to wholeheartedly recommend Claude Code to anyone over the past few months. The tool itself is genuinely excellent (arguably the best in its category), and Anthropic's models are still some of the most impressive out there when it comes to coding. Unfortunately, being the best tool in a category doesn't automatically make it the right tool to recommend, and we are where we are now.

item.title: Windows 11's Low Latency Profile isn't innovation, it's Microsoft finally catching up to its rivals
item.itunes:title: Windows 11's Low Latency Profile isn't innovation, it's Microsoft finally catching up to its rivals
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
There's been quite a bit of talk about Microsoft's blunders with Windows 11, which have led to a rise in criticism and many users going so far as switching to Linux on their computers. But to Microsoft's credit, the company recently began acknowledging many of the problems with Windows and has promised to address them.

One such way that's happening is with the addition of what's called a "Low Latency Profile", which aims to speed up the computer for priority tasks like launching apps, speeding things up across the board. The feature is already available for Windows Insiders, and it's expected to roll out soon for Windows users in general. But what does it actually do?

Flexing the CPU

The speed you need

In order to achieve the faster perceived speeds, Windows 11's "Low Latency Profile" essentially consists of speeding up the CPU very briefly in order to launch the app more quickly. Typically, the CPU may take a while to ramp up for a given workload, so when you launch an app, it might finish loading before the CPU ramps up fully, leading to a slower launch. By boosting the CPU faster and slowing it back down, Windows can remove some brief slowdowns and provide a slightly smoother experience.

You can easily see the difference in what's happening to the CPU as you launch different built-in apps on your system. I tested it with a handful of apps such as File Explorer, Settings, Control Panel, and a couple others, and with the low-latency profile enabled, you can see noticeably higher spikes in CPU usage in Task Manager (in the image above) compared to the spikes without this feature, shown below.

Notably, when I attempted to run multiple tasks at once using a batch script, the CPU usage spike was actually fairly similar regardless of whether I used the low-latency profile or not, so the difference may not be visible in every scenario, though on another retry, there was a visible difference. But for regular day-to-day use, launching one app at a time, you should notice things being slightly snappier.

"Apple does this and y’all love it" — Microsoft fights back over claims its performance boost is lazy

Opening apps and the Start menu should get snappier in a future update.

3
By  Simon Batt

Windows isn't doing anything new

Other platforms already do this

Given that many of the complaints around Windows 11 point to a lack of optimization, simply ramping up the CPU for basic tasks may seem like brute-forcing the problem. And that may be true, if other changes aren't also made, but it's also true that other computer platforms, specifically macOS, already do this.

On macOS, though, I ran a similar test, opening a handful of basic built-in apps on my Mac Mini, and you can see the same kind of spikes, particularly when opening Finder or System Settings. Lighter apps such as Calculator didn't prompt as much of a spike, but looking at the graph in Activity Monitor, you can see the variation.

Depending on your distro, this may also be the case for Linux, though I wasn't able to verify this on my pure Arch install. Linux gives you a lot more freedom with things like CPU governors and schedulers, so your mileage can vary a lot more.

Windows ReadyBoost uses a USB drive to increase performance, but most people shouldn't use it

A great idea, outdone by modern tech

By  João Carrasqueira

Does it actually make a difference?

And does it hurt battery life?

There are good reasons to be excited about this change, but if you're running to measure the performance difference between having this feature enabled or disabled, it may not be immediately obvious. It can be hard to measure these micro performance boosts, but as you use your computer, you'll likely notice things feel smoother overall and apps load quickly.

But it's also possible you're worried about battery life, as many people have come out to comment on the addition of this capability saying that it will just cause laptops to last even less time on battery power. After all, speeding up the processor has to mean using more energy, right? Well, yes and no.

Deals

Shop computer deals and score savings for snappier PCs

Find discounts on laptops, SSDs, RAM, cooling, and peripherals that help cut app-launch lag. Explore Computers & Work Setup deals to unlock savings on upgrades, accessories, and performance gear that make daily use feel snappier.
Deals Explore Computers & Work Setup Deals

Naturally, running the CPU at a higher speed does come with an increase in power consumption, but you have to remember these are extremely short bursts of speed. The goal is exclusively to speed up the feeling of launching apps, which usually happens in less than a second, maybe two. Your CPU will be boosting very briefly to provide a smoother response, but it quickly comes back down to normal speeds. We're not talking about a persistent increase in CPU energy consumption.

Modern Standby is draining your Windows 11 laptop battery, and Microsoft won't fix it

The crux of all Windows laptops

1
By  João Carrasqueira

It's also good to remember that, as mentioned above, Windows is not the only platform that does this, and users seem to be fine with it on macOS and other platforms. There's no reason to be worried about that here.

A lot more work needs to be done

The addition of this capability is undoubtedly good for Windows 11 users who want the experience to feel as snappy as the competitors, but it's also something of a Band-Aid on an open wound unless Microsoft puts in the work to improve other parts of Windows 11. Thankfully, that seems to be the case, as the company is also planning to convert more Windows components into native frameworks, including the Start menu, and potentially even some of the built-in apps. If it follows through on its promises, we could be looking at a far better Windows 11 in a few months' time.
item.description:
There's been quite a bit of talk about Microsoft's blunders with Windows 11, which have led to a rise in criticism and many users going so far as switching to Linux on their computers. But to Microsoft's credit, the company recently began acknowledging many of the problems with Windows and has promised to address them.

One such way that's happening is with the addition of what's called a "Low Latency Profile", which aims to speed up the computer for priority tasks like launching apps, speeding things up across the board. The feature is already available for Windows Insiders, and it's expected to roll out soon for Windows users in general. But what does it actually do?

Flexing the CPU

The speed you need

In order to achieve the faster perceived speeds, Windows 11's "Low Latency Profile" essentially consists of speeding up the CPU very briefly in order to launch the app more quickly. Typically, the CPU may take a while to ramp up for a given workload, so when you launch an app, it might finish loading before the CPU ramps up fully, leading to a slower launch. By boosting the CPU faster and slowing it back down, Windows can remove some brief slowdowns and provide a slightly smoother experience.

You can easily see the difference in what's happening to the CPU as you launch different built-in apps on your system. I tested it with a handful of apps such as File Explorer, Settings, Control Panel, and a couple others, and with the low-latency profile enabled, you can see noticeably higher spikes in CPU usage in Task Manager (in the image above) compared to the spikes without this feature, shown below.

Notably, when I attempted to run multiple tasks at once using a batch script, the CPU usage spike was actually fairly similar regardless of whether I used the low-latency profile or not, so the difference may not be visible in every scenario, though on another retry, there was a visible difference. But for regular day-to-day use, launching one app at a time, you should notice things being slightly snappier.

"Apple does this and y’all love it" — Microsoft fights back over claims its performance boost is lazy

Opening apps and the Start menu should get snappier in a future update.

3

It can be hard to measure these micro performance boosts, but as you use your computer, you'll likely notice things feel smoother overall and apps load quickly.

But it's also possible you're worried about battery life, as many people have come out to comment on the addition of this capability saying that it will just cause laptops to last even less time on battery power. After all, speeding up the processor has to mean using more energy, right? Well, yes and no.

Naturally, running the CPU at a higher speed does come with an increase in power consumption, but you have to remember these are extremely short bursts of speed. The goal is exclusively to speed up the feeling of launching apps, which usually happens in less than a second, maybe two. Your CPU will be boosting very briefly to provide a smoother response, but it quickly comes back down to normal speeds. We're not talking about a persistent increase in CPU energy consumption.

The crux of all Windows laptops

By  João Carrasqueira

It's also good to remember that, as mentioned above, Windows is not the only platform that does this, and users seem to be fine with it on macOS and other platforms. There's no reason to be worried about that here.

A lot more work needs to be done

The addition of this capability is undoubtedly good for Windows 11 users who want the experience to feel as snappy as the competitors, but it's also something of a Band-Aid on an open wound unless Microsoft puts in the work to improve other parts of Windows 11. Thankfully, that seems to be the case, as the company is also planning to convert more Windows components into native frameworks, including the Start menu, and potentially even some of the built-in apps. If it follows through on its promises, we could be looking at a far better Windows 11 in a few months' time.
